questions.questionText: Down syndrome is most commonly caused by:
questions.explanation: Trisomy 21 due to meiotic nondisjunction is the most common cause.
47, XXY is the classic karyotype for Klinefelter syndrome.

questions.questionText: In DNA, adenine pairs with thymine via:
questions.explanation: A-T base pairing involves 2 hydrogen bonds; G-C involves 3.

questions.questionText: The process of synthesizing RNA from a DNA template is called:
questions.explanation: Transcription is RNA synthesis catalyzed by RNA polymerase.
Missense mutations substitute one amino acid for another.

questions.questionText: Which phase of the cell cycle involves DNA replication?
questions.explanation: DNA synthesis occurs during the S (synthetic) phase.

questions.questionText: Turner syndrome usually presents with which karyotype?
questions.explanation: Monosomy X is the hallmark of Turner syndrome.

questions.questionText: The anticodon is found on which molecule?
questions.explanation: tRNA has an anticodon that base pairs with the mRNA codon.

questions.questionText: Huntington's disease exhibits a genetic phenomenon where the age of onset decreases in successive generations. This is called:
Prader-Willi and Angelman syndromes are classic examples of:
questions.explanation: Imprinting involves differential expression depending on the parent of origin.

questions.questionText: In Genetics and Molecular Biology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Anatomy texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Anatomy references for Genetics and Molecular Biology. Subject: Anatomy. Chapter: Genetics and Molecular Biology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Genetics and Molecular Biology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Anatomy. Chapter: Genetics and Molecular Biology.
